topics.title: TypeScript vs JavaScript
topics.summary: Superset of JS — static typing, compile-time checks, better tooling. Compiles to plain JS.
topics.explanation: TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft. Key differences: 1) Static typing: TS catches type errors at compile time, not at runtime. 2) Type inference: TS can infer types from context — you don't always need explicit annotations. 3) Interfaces & types: structural type system — if a value has the right shape, it's compatible. 4) No runtime overhead: TypeScript compiles to plain JavaScript — all type information is erased. 5) Optional: you can adopt TS gradually — .js files work fine alongside .ts files. 6) Tooling: IDEs (VS Code) get autocomplete, refactoring, and error highlights because they know the types.
topics.analogy: TypeScript is JavaScript with a spell-checker that runs before you publish: it catches 'you passed a string where a number goes' at compile time, then strips all the type notes away and ships plain JS.
keyPoints: A statically-typed superset of JS — all JS is valid TS.
Catches type errors at compile time, not in production.
Type inference means you don't annotate everything.
Types are fully erased — zero runtime overhead.
Adoptable gradually (allowJs); huge tooling/autocomplete wins.

situations.question: A teammate argues TypeScript adds too much boilerplate and slows development. How do you respond?
situations.answer: Address the common misconceptions: 1) Type inference eliminates most annotations — const user = { name: 'Alice', age: 30 } needs no explicit type. 2) The initial setup cost (tsconfig, types for libraries) pays back quickly: autocomplete navigates codebases instantly, 'Find all references' works reliably, renames are safe. 3) TypeScript actually speeds up development because you catch errors in the IDE instead of during testing or production. 4) Gradual adoption: start with 'strict: false', add 'allowJs: true' — .js files still work. 5) Real data: Microsoft, Airbnb, Slack, and most large JS codebases have migrated to TS. The argument fails in practice — the initial learning curve is real, but the productivity gains in teams > 2 people are consistently positive.

topics.title: Types, Interfaces & Type Aliases
topics.summary: interface vs type — structural typing, declaration merging, when to use which.
topics.explanation: TypeScript has two ways to define object shapes: interface and type alias. Both describe the structure of objects. Key differences: 1) Interfaces can be extended with 'extends' and merged via declaration merging (two interface declarations with the same name merge their properties). 2) Type aliases use '=' and can represent unions, intersections, primitives, tuples — anything. 3) interfaces are open (can be added to); types are closed (final). Rule of thumb: use 'interface' for public API shapes (especially in libraries — consumers can extend them). Use 'type' for unions, intersections, function types, and complex computed types.
topics.analogy: interface vs type is like a blueprint vs a label: a blueprint (interface) can be extended and added to over time; a label (type) can describe anything — unions, tuples, functions — but is final once written.
keyPoints: Both describe object shapes; TS uses structural ('duck') typing.
interface can be extended and declaration-merged.
type can express unions, intersections, tuples, function types.
Use interface for public/extendable API shapes.
Use type for unions, intersections, and computed types.

situations.question: When would you choose 'type' over 'interface' and vice versa? Give concrete examples.
situations.answer: Use 'interface' when: 1) Defining object shapes for a public library API — consumers can augment with declaration merging: interface MyLibConfig { timeout: number } → user adds their own property. 2) Class implements — classes implement interfaces naturally. 3) When you want to communicate 'this is a contract'. Use 'type' when: 1) You need a union: type Status = 'pending' | 'success' | 'error'. 2) You need an intersection: type EnhancedUser = User & Auditable. 3) Function signatures: type Handler = (req: Request, res: Response) => void. 4) Mapped/conditional types — only possible with type alias. 5) Aliases for primitives: type UserId = string. In practice: many teams just pick one and stay consistent. The important thing is knowing the differences when they actually matter (declaration merging being the biggest gotcha).

topics.title: Primitive & Special Types
topics.summary: unknown vs any vs never. void, null, undefined. Literal types. Type narrowing.
topics.explanation: TypeScript's special types: 1) 'any' — opt-out of type checking entirely. Avoid — it silently disables safety for that variable and anything it touches. 2) 'unknown' — the safe alternative to 'any'. You MUST narrow the type before using it. 3) 'never' — a value that never exists. Used for: unreachable code, exhaustive checks, functions that always throw. 4) 'void' — a function return type meaning 'no return value'. Different from 'undefined'. 5) Literal types: const mode: 'light' | 'dark' = 'light' — not just 'string', but specifically one of those values. 6) Type narrowing: using typeof, instanceof, in, or custom type guards to tell TS which specific type a variable is in a given code branch.
topics.analogy: any is taking the batteries out of the smoke detector — silence, but no safety. unknown is the smoke detector that makes you prove it's safe (narrow) before you proceed.
keyPoints: any disables checking and infects everything it touches — avoid it.
unknown is the safe any: you must narrow before using it.
never = a value that can't exist (used for exhaustiveness checks).
void = a function returns nothing meaningful.
Literal types pin a value to exact options ('light' | 'dark').

situations.question: You receive data from an API and don't know its exact shape at compile time. Should you type it as 'any' or 'unknown'? What's your approach?
situations.answer: Always 'unknown', never 'any' for external data. With 'any': the bug silently propagates — you access data.user.name and TS won't warn if the API changes and name becomes username. With 'unknown': TS forces you to validate before use. Best approach: 1) Use a runtime validation library: Zod, Valibot, or io-ts. Write a schema and parse the response: const user = UserSchema.parse(await res.json()). If parsing fails, you get a clear error immediately, not a silent undefined 3 levels deep. 2) If you must type manually: use a type assertion with a comment explaining why: const data = await res.json() as UserResponse; // validated by backend contract. 3) Never cast unknown → specific type without validation in production code. The rule: 'unknown' forces the burden of proof onto you, which is exactly what you want for external data.

topics.title: Generic Functions & Constraints
topics.summary: Write once, work for any type — with constraints to ensure the type has what you need.
topics.explanation: Generics allow you to write functions, classes, and interfaces that work with any type while preserving type safety. Without generics you'd need 'any' or overloads for every type. Key concepts: 1) Type parameters: <T> is a placeholder resolved at call site. 2) Constraints: 'extends' limits what T can be — T extends string means T must be string or a subtype. 3) Multiple type params: <T, U> for functions relating two types. 4) Default type params: <T = string>. 5) Bounded generics: <T extends { length: number }> means 'any type with a length property'.
topics.analogy: Generics are a one-size-fits-all container that remembers what you put in: put in a number, get a number back — type safety preserved, no need for a separate box per type.
keyPoints: <T> is a type placeholder resolved at the call site.
Preserves the input→output type relationship (vs any).
Constrain with extends (<T extends { length: number }>).
keyof constraints make property access type-safe.
Support defaults (<T = string>) and multiple params (<T, U>).

situations.question: You're building a reusable data fetching hook that works for any API endpoint and returns typed data. How do you type it with generics?
situations.answer: Generic hook with type parameter: function useFetch<T>(url: string): { data: T | null; loading: boolean; error: Error | null }. The caller provides the type: const { data } = useFetch<User>('/api/user/1') — data is typed as User | null. For better ergonomics with Zod: function useFetch<T>(url: string, schema: z.ZodType<T>) — the schema both validates runtime data and provides the T type automatically. The caller: const { data } = useFetch('/api/user/1', UserSchema) — T is inferred from the schema. No need to write the type twice. This pattern (generic + runtime validation) is used in TanStack Query's queryFn pattern and is the production-grade approach.

topics.title: Infer & Generic Patterns
topics.summary: ReturnType, Parameters, infer keyword — extracting types from existing types.
topics.explanation: Advanced generic patterns let you derive types from existing code automatically, keeping types in sync without duplication. Key utilities: 1) ReturnType<T>: extracts the return type of a function. 2) Parameters<T>: extracts the parameters as a tuple. 3) InstanceType<T>: extracts the instance type of a class. 4) 'infer' keyword: used inside conditional types to capture/extract a type into a variable. These are heavily used in library type definitions to ensure the types they expose stay in sync with their implementations automatically.
topics.analogy: infer is a fill-in-the-blank for types: 'if T looks like Promise<___>, capture the blank as U'. ReturnType/Parameters use it to read types straight off your existing functions, so they never drift out of sync.
keyPoints: ReturnType<T> / Parameters<T> derive types from functions.
infer captures a type from inside a pattern in a conditional type.
Awaited<T> unwraps a (possibly nested) Promise.
Lets types stay in sync with implementation automatically.
Backbone of library typings (tRPC, Prisma).

situations.question: Your team defines API functions in one file. Other files import the functions and need their return types. How do you keep these types in sync without duplication?
situations.answer: Use ReturnType<typeof fn>: 1) Define your API functions normally — no separate type file needed. 2) In consuming files: import { getUser } from './api'; type User = Awaited<ReturnType<typeof getUser>>. The 'Awaited' unwraps the Promise. 3) If the API function's return changes, every consuming type updates automatically — no manual sync needed. Pattern: // api.ts export async function getUser(id: string) { return db.user.findUnique({ where: { id } }); } // elsewhere: import type { getUser } from './api'; type User = Awaited<ReturnType<typeof getUser>>. This is the pattern used by tRPC and Prisma — your DB schema or API schema IS the type, no duplication needed.

topics.title: Built-in Utility Types
topics.summary: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly — transform types without duplication.
topics.explanation: TypeScript includes built-in utility types that transform existing types. These eliminate the need to manually define variations of your types. Key utilities: Partial<T> makes all properties optional. Required<T> makes all properties required. Readonly<T> prevents mutation. Pick<T, K> selects specific properties. Omit<T, K> removes specific properties. Record<K, V> creates a dictionary type. Exclude<T, U> removes union members. Extract<T, U> keeps only matching union members. NonNullable<T> removes null and undefined.
topics.analogy: Utility types are photo filters for types: Partial makes everything optional, Pick crops to a few fields, Omit removes some — all from one original, so there's a single source of truth.
keyPoints: Partial/Required toggle optionality; Readonly prevents mutation.
Pick selects fields; Omit removes fields.
Record<K,V> builds a typed dictionary.
Exclude/Extract/NonNullable manipulate union members.
Compose them (Partial<Pick<User,…>>) instead of redefining types.

situations.question: You have a large User interface but your update endpoint should only accept a subset of fields and all optional. How do you type the request body?
situations.answer: Compose utilities: type UpdateUserBody = Partial<Pick<User, 'name' | 'email' | 'age'>>. Pick selects the updatable fields, Partial makes them all optional. For forms: type UserFormState = Partial<Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>> — remove system-managed fields, make rest optional (for step-by-step form completion). For API layers: type CreateUser = Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'updatedAt'> — system assigns these. type UpdateUser = Partial<CreateUser>. Never manually redefine these — if User changes, all derived types update automatically. This is the main value of utility types: single source of truth.

topics.title: Readonly & Const Assertions
topics.summary: 'as const' for literal inference, Readonly for immutable objects, ReadonlyArray.
topics.explanation: TypeScript has two mechanisms for immutability: 1) Readonly<T> utility type — makes all top-level properties readonly at the type level (deep immutability needs DeepReadonly). 2) 'as const' assertion — freezes a value to its most specific literal type. Without 'as const': const config = { env: 'prod' } has type { env: string }. With 'as const': type is { readonly env: 'prod' } — the exact literal, not just string. This is critical for objects used as lookup maps or config where you want exhaustive union types derived from the values.
topics.analogy: Without 'as const', TS rounds your value up to a vague type ('prod' becomes just string). 'as const' freezes it to the exact literal, so you can derive precise unions straight from the data.
keyPoints: Readonly<T> makes top-level props immutable (deep needs DeepReadonly).
'as const' freezes a value to its narrowest literal type.
Derive unions from objects: typeof OBJ[keyof typeof OBJ].
ReadonlyArray blocks mutating methods (push/splice).
Single source of truth — add a value, the union updates.

situations.question: You define a THEME object with color values but TypeScript infers the type as 'string' instead of the literal color values. How do you fix this so you can derive the exact union type?
situations.answer: Use 'as const': const THEME = { primary: '#6366f1', secondary: '#8b5cf6', danger: '#ef4444' } as const. Now: type ThemeColor = typeof THEME[keyof typeof THEME] gives '#6366f1' | '#8b5cf6' | '#ef4444' as a union — not just 'string'. This matters when: 1) You want to type a function that only accepts valid theme colors — function applyColor(color: ThemeColor). 2) You want exhaustive checks — a switch on ThemeColor gets an error if you miss a case. 3) You want autocomplete when calling applyColor() — the IDE shows only the exact valid values. Without 'as const' you'd have to maintain the union manually alongside the object, creating two places to update.

topics.title: Type Guards
topics.summary: typeof, instanceof, in, discriminated unions, custom type predicates — is operator.
topics.explanation: Type narrowing is how TypeScript refines a broad type (union) to a specific type within a conditional block. Built-in narrowing: typeof (primitive checks), instanceof (class checks), 'in' (property existence). Custom type guards: functions that return 'value is Type' — a type predicate. TypeScript tracks narrowing through the control flow — after 'if (typeof x === string)', TS knows x is string in that block. Discriminated unions: add a 'kind' or 'type' literal field to each union member, then switch on it — TypeScript narrows to the exact member in each case.
topics.analogy: Narrowing is a series of doors that TS watches you walk through: after 'if (typeof x === string)', it knows x is a string in that room. A discriminated union puts a labeled tag on each door so the switch is exhaustive.
keyPoints: Built-in narrowing: typeof, instanceof, the 'in' operator.
Discriminated unions: a shared literal 'kind' field + switch.
Custom guards return `value is Type` (type predicates).
Assertion functions (`asserts x is T`) narrow for the rest of the scope.
A `never` default case enforces exhaustive handling at compile time.

situations.question: You have a function that receives a value that could be a string, number, or a custom Error object. How do you handle each case with full type safety?
situations.answer: Discriminated union + type predicates: 1) If you control the types, add a kind field: type Result = { kind: 'string'; value: string } | { kind: 'number'; value: number } | { kind: 'error'; error: CustomError } — switch on kind for full narrowing. 2) If the types are external (no kind field), use type predicates: function isCustomError(v: unknown): v is CustomError { return v instanceof CustomError } — then chain: if (typeof val === 'string') { ... } else if (typeof val === 'number') { ... } else if (isCustomError(val)) { ... }. 3) Key insight: TypeScript's control flow analysis tracks what you've ruled out. After 'if (typeof val === string) return' — in the rest of the function, TS knows val is NOT string. Use this with exhaustive never checks to ensure all cases are handled at compile time.

topics.title: Assertion Functions & satisfies
topics.summary: 'satisfies' operator — validate without widening. Assertion functions that narrow after call.
topics.explanation: Two newer TypeScript features for type safety: 1) 'satisfies' operator (TS 4.9): validates that a value matches a type WITHOUT widening the type. Unlike a type annotation (which loses literal types), 'satisfies' keeps the inferred literal type while still checking the constraint. 2) Assertion functions: functions with 'asserts value is Type' in their return type. After calling them, TypeScript permanently narrows the type for the rest of the scope — unlike type predicates (which only narrow inside the if block).
topics.analogy: 'satisfies' is a fitting room that checks the outfit fits the dress code WITHOUT swapping your clothes for a generic uniform: the value is validated but keeps its exact literal types.
keyPoints: satisfies validates a value against a type without widening it.
Type annotation widens (loses literals); satisfies keeps them.
Great for config objects, route maps, palettes.
Assertion functions narrow permanently after the call (vs guards: only in the if).
Combine both for validated-yet-precise config.

situations.question: You're building a theme config object. You want autocomplete for the exact color values you defined, but also want TypeScript to warn if you add a property that doesn't match a ColorToken type. How?
situations.answer: Use 'satisfies': const theme = { primary: '#6366f1', secondary: '#8b5cf6' } satisfies Record<string, ColorToken>. Result: 1) TypeScript validates that '#6366f1' is a valid ColorToken — it warns if you put an invalid value. 2) The type of theme.primary is the LITERAL '#6366f1', not just ColorToken — so autocomplete and exactness are preserved. 3) If you write const theme: Record<string, ColorToken> = {...} instead, theme.primary is just ColorToken — you lose the literal type and any narrowing benefits. The 'satisfies' pattern is especially powerful for config objects, route maps, and any object where you want both constraint checking AND literal type preservation.

topics.title: Class Features
topics.summary: Access modifiers, abstract classes, readonly, parameter properties, class generics.
topics.explanation: TypeScript adds to JavaScript classes: 1) Access modifiers: public (default), protected (class + subclasses), private (class only). Note: JS also has # private fields — TS 'private' is erased at runtime, # is truly private. 2) Parameter properties: shorthand to declare and initialize in constructor: constructor(private name: string) {} is equivalent to declaring private name: string and this.name = name. 3) readonly properties: can only be assigned in constructor. 4) Abstract classes: can't be instantiated — must be extended. Define the interface that subclasses must implement. 5) Class can implement multiple interfaces. 6) Generic classes: class Stack<T> { private items: T[] = []; }
topics.analogy: TS 'private' is a 'Staff Only' sign (a polite compile-time warning, removed at runtime); JS '#private' is a locked door (truly inaccessible even in plain JS).
keyPoints: Modifiers: public (default), protected, private (compile-time only).
'#' fields are runtime-private (hidden from JSON/Reflect).
Parameter properties declare + assign in the constructor in one line.
abstract classes define a contract and can't be instantiated.
Classes can be generic (Stack<T>) and implement interfaces.

situations.question: What's the difference between TypeScript 'private' and JavaScript '#' private fields? When does it matter?
situations.answer: Critical difference: TS 'private' is compile-time only — it's erased in the output JS. '#' is runtime private — truly inaccessible from outside even in plain JS. When it matters: 1) JS consumers: if your TS library is consumed by plain JS code, 'private' fields are accessible at runtime (just without TS warnings). '#' fields are truly inaccessible. 2) Serialization: JSON.stringify can access TS 'private' fields (they're just regular properties). '#' fields are hidden from JSON.stringify. 3) Reflection/proxies: code that uses Object.keys() or Reflect can see TS 'private' fields. 4) Recommendation: use '#' for truly sensitive internal state. Use TS 'private' for most OOP encapsulation where runtime protection isn't critical — the compile-time check catches 99% of misuse in TS codebases. Don't mix: prefer '#' consistently or 'private' consistently in a codebase.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.summary: Class, method, property, parameter decorators. Metadata reflection. Angular/NestJS patterns.
topics.explanation: Decorators are a Stage 3 proposal (ES) and a TypeScript feature (with experimentalDecorators or the new TS 5.0 decorators). They are functions that modify classes, methods, properties, or parameters at definition time. Common uses: logging, dependency injection (Angular, NestJS), ORM mappings (TypeORM), validation, memoization. Two decorator APIs: 1) Legacy (experimentalDecorators: true): widely used by Angular, NestJS, TypeORM. 2) TS 5.0+ standard decorators: new ECMAScript-aligned API with different signature. Most libraries still use legacy decorators.
topics.analogy: Decorators are stamps you press onto a class or method at definition time (@Injectable, @Log) that add behavior or metadata — like a notary stamp that frameworks later read.
keyPoints: Functions that modify classes/methods/properties/params at define-time.
Power Angular DI, NestJS, TypeORM, validation.
Method decorators can wrap behavior (logging, timing).
With reflect-metadata they attach readable metadata.
Two APIs exist: legacy (experimentalDecorators) and TS 5.0 standard.

situations.question: You need to add automatic logging to every method in a class without modifying each method. How do you implement this with decorators?
situations.answer: Class decorator that wraps all methods: function LogAllMethods(target: Function) { for (const key of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(target.prototype)) { if (key === 'constructor') continue; const descriptor = Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(target.prototype, key); if (descriptor && typeof descriptor.value === 'function') { const original = descriptor.value; descriptor.value = function(...args: any[]) { console.log(`[${target.name}.${key}]`, args); const result = original.apply(this, args); console.log(`[${target.name}.${key}] →`, result); return result; }; Object.defineProperty(target.prototype, key, descriptor); } } }. Then: @LogAllMethods class UserService { ... } — all methods auto-logged. Production consideration: use a proper logging library, be careful not to log sensitive data (passwords, tokens), and filter by log level. Also consider: this approach doesn't handle async methods' resolved values correctly — you need to check if result is a Promise and await it.

topics.title: tsconfig.json Deep Dive
topics.summary: strict mode, target, module, paths, composite projects — what each option actually does.
topics.explanation: tsconfig.json controls how TypeScript compiles your code. Key options: 1) strict: true — enables all strict checks (strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, etc). Always enable. 2) target: which JS version to output (ES2020, ESNext). 3) module: how modules are output (CommonJS for Node, ESNext for bundlers). 4) moduleResolution: how imports are resolved (Bundler for modern setups, Node16 for native ESM Node). 5) paths: import aliases (@/ → src/). 6) composite: for project references (monorepos). 7) incremental: faster rebuilds by caching. 8) declaration + declarationMap: generate .d.ts files for libraries.
topics.analogy: tsconfig.json is the control panel for the compiler: it sets how strict the inspector is, which JS version to output, and how imports are resolved. 'strict: true' is the master safety switch you always leave on.
keyPoints: strict: true enables the full suite of safety checks — always on.
target/module/moduleResolution control output and import resolution.
paths sets import aliases (@/ → src/).
noUncheckedIndexedAccess makes index access T | undefined.
incremental + skipLibCheck speed up builds.

situations.question: You enable 'noUncheckedIndexedAccess' and suddenly 100 TypeScript errors appear. What does this option do and how do you handle the migration?
situations.answer: noUncheckedIndexedAccess makes array/object index access return T | undefined instead of T. Without it: const arr = [1,2,3]; const first = arr[0]; — first is 'number'. With it: first is 'number | undefined' — forcing you to handle the case where the index might not exist. How to migrate: 1) Use optional chaining: arr[0]?.toString() instead of arr[0].toString(). 2) Destructure with defaults: const [first = 0] = arr. 3) Check first: if (arr[0] !== undefined) { arr[0].toString(); }. 4) For maps/records: const val = myRecord[key]; — now val is V | undefined, forcing you to handle missing keys. The 100 errors are exposing real potential bugs. Fix them properly — don't just add ! non-null assertions everywhere. This option is highly recommended for new projects but painful to add to existing codebases.

topics.title: Declaration Files (.d.ts)
topics.summary: Augmenting modules, global types, writing .d.ts for JS libraries, triple-slash references.
topics.explanation: Declaration files (.d.ts) contain only type information — no runtime code. Uses: 1) Type definitions for JS libraries (DefinitelyTyped / @types packages). 2) Module augmentation: add types to an existing module (e.g., add custom properties to Express Request). 3) Global augmentation: add to the global namespace (e.g., add to Window). 4) Ambient declarations: declare global variables that exist in the environment (injected by bundler, etc). Writing your own: when building a library, set 'declaration: true' in tsconfig — TypeScript auto-generates .d.ts from your .ts files.
topics.analogy: .d.ts files are type-only instruction manuals with no working parts: they tell TS the shape of JS libraries or let you bolt extra typed properties onto existing things (like req.user on Express).
keyPoints: Contain types only — no runtime code.
@types/* packages provide types for untyped JS libraries.
Module augmentation adds types to an existing module.
Global augmentation extends Window/global namespace.
declaration: true auto-generates .d.ts for your library.

situations.question: You're using an npm package that has no TypeScript types. How do you add types without modifying the library?
situations.answer: Three options, best to worst: 1) Check DefinitelyTyped: npm install -D @types/package-name — the community may have already typed it. 2) Create a local .d.ts file: create src/types/package-name.d.ts. Declare what you need: declare module 'package-name' { export function doThing(x: string): number; }. You only need to type what you actually use. 3) Quick escape hatch (avoid): declare module 'package-name' — this types the entire module as 'any'. Functional but you lose all safety. For option 2, put the file in a location included by tsconfig and make sure typeRoots or include covers it. If you type the whole library properly, consider publishing it to DefinitelyTyped to help the community.

topics.title: Builder & Fluent API Patterns
topics.summary: Method chaining with precise return types. Builder accumulates type information at each step.
topics.explanation: The Builder pattern in TypeScript can be enhanced with generics to track what has been set. A 'Fluent Builder' returns 'this' from each method — enabling chaining. An advanced 'Type-Safe Builder' uses generic type parameters to track which fields have been set, making the 'build()' method only available when all required fields are provided — a compile-time check rather than a runtime error.
topics.analogy: A fluent builder is ordering at a sandwich counter: each step (.where().limit()) adds to your order and hands the counter back to you. A type-safe builder won't let you say 'done' (build()) until the required ingredients are in.
keyPoints: Each method returns `this` to enable chaining.
Accumulates configuration step by step.
Type-safe builders track set fields via generic state.
build() can be gated to require fields at compile time.
Used by query builders, AWS CDK, SDK configs.

situations.question: You're building an SDK where users chain method calls to configure a request. How do you ensure they can't forget a required method (like setApiKey()) at compile time?
situations.answer: Type-safe builder with generic state tracking: 1) Define a State type with boolean flags for each required config: type SDKState = { apiKey: boolean; endpoint: boolean }. 2) Builder class is generic on State extends SDKState. 3) Each required setter method returns a narrowed type: setApiKey(key): Builder<State & { apiKey: true }>. 4) The build/execute method has a 'this' constraint: build(this: Builder<{ apiKey: true; endpoint: true }>): SDK. 5) Now: new SDKBuilder().setApiKey('key').build() — ❌ compile error because endpoint isn't set. new SDKBuilder().setApiKey('key').setEndpoint('https://...').build() — ✅. This pattern gives users clear compile-time feedback without any runtime validation overhead. Used by AWS CDK and some query builders.

topics.title: Result Types & Error Handling
topics.summary: Result<T, E> pattern, Option/Maybe type — typed errors without exceptions.
topics.explanation: Instead of throwing exceptions (which are untyped in TypeScript — any function can throw anything), the Result pattern makes errors explicit in the return type. Inspired by Rust's Result<T, E>. A function returns either Ok(value) or Err(error) — callers must handle both. This makes error handling visible, type-safe, and impossible to forget. Libraries: neverthrow, ts-results. The pattern works especially well with discriminated unions, which TypeScript narrows perfectly.
topics.analogy: The Result pattern is returning a labeled box that says either 'success: here's the data' or 'failure: here's why' — instead of throwing an unlabeled error nobody is forced to catch.
keyPoints: Errors become values: Result<T,E> = Ok<T> | Err<E>.
Callers must handle both branches — errors can't be forgotten.
Throws are untyped in TS; Result makes failures explicit.
Pairs perfectly with discriminated-union narrowing.
Libraries: neverthrow, ts-results (Rust-inspired).

situations.question: Your team uses try/catch everywhere and errors are untyped (catch (e) gives 'unknown'). How do you introduce typed error handling?
situations.answer: Incremental migration: 1) Create a Result type and a tryCatch wrapper: function tryCatch<T>(fn: () => T): Result<T, Error> { try { return ok(fn()); } catch(e) { return err(e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e))); } }. 2) Start wrapping boundary functions first (API calls, DB queries) — these are highest risk and most benefit from typed errors. 3) Use neverthrow library for a production-ready implementation with .map(), .mapErr(), .andThen() chaining. 4) For 'catch (e)': always narrow first: if (e instanceof Error) { e.message } — never cast 'e' directly to Error, it might not be. 5) Long term: move errors from throw to return values at integration points. Internal pure functions can still throw — they're easier to reason about. The pattern pays off most in API layers, authentication, and I/O operations where errors are expected and varied.

topics.title: Conditional Types
topics.summary: T extends U ? X : Y — type-level if/else. Distributive conditionals. infer in conditions.
topics.explanation: Conditional types are the type-level equivalent of ternary expressions: T extends U ? X : Y. They are evaluated at compile time by TypeScript. Key behaviors: 1) Distributive: when T is a union, the conditional is applied to each member separately. 2) 'infer' inside conditional: extract/capture a type from the pattern being matched. 3) Naked type parameter: distributivity only applies when the type parameter is 'naked' (not wrapped in another type). Wrapping in a tuple ([T] extends [U]) disables distributivity. Used heavily in built-in utility types like ReturnType, Parameters, Awaited.
topics.analogy: A conditional type is an if/else at the type level: 'T extends string ? X : Y'. Over a union it's applied to each member separately — like running the check on every item in a list.
keyPoints: T extends U ? X : Y — type-level ternary, evaluated at compile time.
Distributive over unions when T is 'naked'.
Wrap in a tuple ([T] extends [U]) to disable distribution.
infer extracts a captured type from the matched pattern.
Underlies ReturnType, Parameters, Awaited.

situations.question: You need a type that, given a function type, extracts the type of its first parameter only. How do you write it?
situations.answer: Use conditional types with infer and tuple destructuring: type FirstParam<T extends (...args: any[]) => any> = T extends (first: infer F, ...rest: any[]) => any ? F : never. Example: function login(email: string, password: string): void {} type EmailType = FirstParam<typeof login>; // string. For a more general 'nth parameter': type NthParam<T extends (...args: any[]) => any, N extends number> = Parameters<T>[N]. Parameters<T> gives a tuple of all params, then index with N. This is cleaner for fixed positions. The conditional type approach is better when you need to match a specific structural pattern, not just index into a known tuple.

topics.title: Mapped Types & Template Literal Types
topics.summary: { [K in keyof T]: ... } — transform every property. as clause remapping. Template literals.
topics.explanation: Mapped types iterate over the keys of a type and produce a new type by transforming each key/value. Syntax: { [K in keyof T]: NewType }. Modifiers: '+?' or '-?' to add/remove optional, '+readonly' or '-readonly'. Key remapping with 'as': { [K in keyof T as NewKey]: T[K] } — can filter or rename keys. Template literal types (TS 4.1+) allow string manipulation at the type level: type Getters<T> = { [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K] }.
topics.analogy: A mapped type is .map() for an object's keys: walk every property and transform it. Template literal types are string math on top — turning 'name' into 'getName'.
keyPoints: { [K in keyof T]: … } transforms every property.
Modifiers +?/-? and +readonly/-readonly add or strip flags.
Key remapping with 'as' renames or filters keys.
Template literal types build/transform string keys.
Filter with `as (cond ? K : never)` to drop properties.

situations.question: You have an interface with 20 fields. You need to create a companion type where every field is wrapped in an Observable<T>. How do you do this without duplicating the interface?
situations.answer: Mapped type: type Observables<T> = { [K in keyof T]: Observable<T[K]> }. Usage: type UserObservables = Observables<User> — produces { id: Observable<string>; name: Observable<string>; ... }. If User changes, UserObservables updates automatically. Can combine with filtering: type ObservableStrings<T> = { [K in keyof T as T[K] extends string ? K : never]: Observable<T[K]> } — only wrap string properties. This pattern is used heavily in RxJS state management (NgRx, Akita) and in React's useSelectorAll patterns. Key insight: mapped types are the TypeScript equivalent of array .map() — applied to the structure of a type rather than the values of an array.

topics.title: Declaration Merging & Module Augmentation
topics.summary: Extend third-party types, augment global/module namespaces, interface merging mechanics.
topics.explanation: Declaration merging allows multiple declarations with the same name to merge into a single definition. This is the mechanism behind: augmenting Express Request with user data, adding methods to built-in prototypes (safely in .d.ts), extending global namespace. Rules: interfaces merge property sets (must be compatible). Namespaces can merge with interfaces, classes, functions. Module augmentation: declare module 'module-name' { } inside a .ts file adds types to that module. Global augmentation: declare global { } adds to the global namespace. Note: module augmentation only works in module files (files with import/export).
topics.analogy: Declaration merging is two people adding pages to the same binder labeled 'Window' — TS staples them into one. It's how you bolt typed extras (req.user) onto libraries you don't own.
keyPoints: Same-name interfaces merge their property sets.
Module augmentation adds types to an existing module.
Global augmentation extends the global namespace.
Only works inside module files (with import/export).
Use carefully when augmenting built-ins like Array.

situations.question: You use Fastify and add a custom 'user' property to the request via middleware. How do you type this so every route handler gets correct types for request.user?
situations.answer: Fastify supports TypeScript generic decorators: 1) Declare the augmented request type: interface FastifyRequest { user: AuthenticatedUser; } — but Fastify uses generics, not augmentation. 2) Better: use Fastify's TypeScript support with augmentation on FastifyRequest: declare module 'fastify' { interface FastifyRequest { user: AuthenticatedUser; } }. 3) In your authentication hook: fastify.addHook('onRequest', async (request) => { request.user = await verifyToken(request.headers.authorization); }). 4) Now in all route handlers: request.user.id — fully typed. 5) Make user optional on the base interface (user?: AuthenticatedUser) and required in a Protected route type — so public routes don't falsely claim user is defined. Pattern: AuthenticatedRequest extends FastifyRequest { user: AuthenticatedUser } — use this as the request type for protected routes only.

topics.title: Type-Level Performance
topics.summary: Expensive types, type instantiation depth, lazy evaluation — avoiding 'type instantiation is excessively deep'.
topics.explanation: TypeScript type checking has performance limits. Complex recursive or deeply nested types can cause: 1) 'Type instantiation is excessively deep and possibly infinite' error. 2) Slow IDE (type checking takes seconds). 3) 'tsc' taking minutes. Root causes: deep recursive conditional types, large union/intersection chains, overuse of infer in recursive types. Solutions: 1) Add bounds to recursive types. 2) Use interface instead of type for object shapes (interfaces cache better). 3) Break complex types into named intermediate types. 4) Use 'as' assertion when you've proven safety. 5) Enable incremental compilation. 6) Use 'isolatedModules: true' for faster per-file checking.
topics.analogy: Overly clever recursive types are like an inspector who re-checks every nested box infinitely — the build crawls or errors out ('excessively deep'). Bound the recursion and cache named types to keep it fast.
keyPoints: Deep recursive/conditional types can blow up compile time.
Symptoms: 'instantiation excessively deep', slow IDE, slow tsc.
Add depth bounds to recursive types.
Prefer interface over complex type aliases (interfaces cache).
Diagnose with tsc --generateTrace and @typescript/analyze-trace.

situations.question: Your TypeScript compilation went from 5 seconds to 45 seconds after adding a new utility type. How do you diagnose and fix the performance issue?
situations.answer: Diagnosis: 1) Run: npx tsc --generateTrace traceDir — generates a trace file. 2) Open in Chrome DevTools (chrome://tracing) or use @typescript/analyze-trace. 3) Look for types with high 'instantiation count' or 'check time'. 4) VS Code: enable 'TypeScript: Show instantiation counts' in settings — hover over types to see cost. Fixes: 1) Break the complex type into smaller named intermediate types — TS caches named types. 2) Replace recursive conditional types with a bounded depth version. 3) Convert frequently-used object type aliases to interfaces. 4) Use 'as' assertion at a well-understood boundary instead of letting TS infer through complex transformations. 5) Check if a union is exploding combinatorially — flatten it. 6) Enable 'skipLibCheck: true' to skip node_modules .d.ts (if not already). The most common culprit: a deeply recursive mapped or conditional type applied to a large interface.

topics.title: Project References & Monorepos
topics.summary: composite projects, references, incremental builds — structuring large TypeScript codebases.
topics.explanation: TypeScript Project References (TS 3.0+) allow splitting a large codebase into multiple tsconfig projects that reference each other. Benefits: 1) Incremental builds: only rebuild changed projects. 2) Isolation: each package has its own tsconfig, strictness, output. 3) Type-checked boundaries: importing from a referenced project goes through its built .d.ts, not re-checking all source. Setup: each package has 'composite: true' in tsconfig. The root tsconfig has 'references' pointing to each package. Build with 'tsc --build' (tsbuild). Works with pnpm/npm workspaces. Turborepo and Nx use this internally for TS monorepos.
topics.analogy: Project references split a giant codebase into separately-built rooms: change one room and only it gets rebuilt (via its .d.ts), instead of repainting the whole house every time.
keyPoints: Split a codebase into multiple referenced tsconfig projects.
composite: true + declaration: true are required.
Imports go through built .d.ts — no full re-check.
Build with `tsc --build` for incremental rebuilds.
Turborepo/Nx use this for fast TS monorepos.

situations.question: Your monorepo has 15 TypeScript packages and type checking takes 4 minutes. How do you restructure it to speed this up?
situations.answer: Implement TypeScript Project References: 1) Add 'composite: true' and 'declaration: true' to each package's tsconfig. 2) Map the dependency graph — packages/ui depends on packages/tokens, packages/api depends on packages/shared, etc. 3) Add 'references' array to each package's tsconfig pointing to its dependencies. 4) Create a root tsconfig.json with 'files: []' and 'references' to all packages. 5) Build with 'tsc --build' — TS now only rebuilds packages whose sources changed. 6) Add '.tsbuildinfo' files to .gitignore, commit them in CI cache. 7) Set up Turborepo/Nx pipeline: 'tsc --build --noEmit' as the type-check task, with proper cache inputs (src files, tsconfig). 8) In CI: restore the .tsbuildinfo cache — incremental builds skip unchanged packages. Expected improvement: from 4 minutes to <30 seconds for typical incremental builds where only 1-2 packages changed.
